I had misgivings as the low temperatures we had put a skin of ice on most of the ponds. The White Pelican I saw in Harwich on my way to Chatham, but when we returned in pm and waited an hour, it did not appear.  A Virginia Rail fed at our feet for about 1/2 hour as we waited for the Pelican.

Observation Details: Easton pond, Newport. At the suggestion of the leader and the agreement of the group, this BBC walk went to Newport, RI [scheduled for Westport, MA] to see the Smew. Our first stop at Easton Pond did not produce the bird and right away the leader and the group worried about not being able to find it. We waited at Easton Pond for a while and then went to the Norman Bird Sanctuary in Middletown. There we saw the Northern Saw-whet Owl. After birding the sanctuary we went back to Easton Pond but the Smew was not there. We waited for a while and then decided to leave. Just as half the group began to climb down the embankment, Brad Blodget yelled "There it is!"" and everyone scrambled back up to see it as it flew in and landed on the pond."
Checklist Comments: Brookline Bird Club field card data. Trip Leader: Robert J. O'Hara. One of the most exciting BBC trips I have led!

Observation Details: Seen at Cumberland Farms Dairy Farm in Bridgewater by leader and 3 members. This bird has a white eyering, a light buffy shade to breast (not striped as with Lapland), slightly smaller than Lapland. At times the bird would spread its tail and show the characteristic black T pattern on its white tail. This bird has been seen by many birders for the past few days. Seen within 20 feed with 34 Lapland Longspurs.

BBC Trip, Leader: Glenn d'Entremont; Extension of Monomoy trip; LIFE Bird for BBC, trip officially extended to Wellfleet, 9 of 12 original participants present, Spotted Redshank. Most of body was black/dark with white blotches on belly and lower breast. Legs were burnt orange with redish highlights. Base of lower mandible and 3/4 of lower bandible red. Approx. the same size as Greater Yellowlegs.  Feeding habits - put bill in water and moved head side to side, not up and down, similar to Avocet feeding.

BBC Trip,  Leader: Marjorie Rines.   Rainy.  I used to lead trips for the BBC Trip,  in May in the Middlesex Fels, and year after year it would pour rain on the day of the trip. (there is more than one member of this list who will remember this well), and I gradually began to think of myself as the Raining Queen of Medford.  Well, today's trip was at Horn Pond, and at 7am it was pouring.  I entertained the hope that no one would show up, but of the 10 people who signed up for the trip, 7 showed up including one without a rain jacket who optimistically decided it was going to clear up.   Despite the rain (and mosquitos) we had a pretty fine trip. My personal highlight was watching an adult male orchard oriole feeding two fledglings perched in the same tree.  As one participant said, An A+ experience.

BBC Pelagic trip departed at 4:00 and headed out along the east side of Nantucket to Veatch Canyon. Lat/Long taken at midpoint of transect. WEATHER: Fairly cool (65-70 F) early, SW at 10, and skies partly cloudy.

Observation Details: Two-day total of 10 (A high total; see notes above)
Checklist Comments: Full day total for entire boat for 28 August. No one person saw the entire list of species or complete counts; official tally kept by Rick Heil. OTHER FAUNA: MAMMALS: Fin Whale - 5 (two-day total 23); Risso's Dolohin - 12 (two-day total 12); Atlantic Spotted Dolphin 60 (RARE!; two-day total 60); Bottlenosed Dolphin - 8 (two-day total 8); dolphin sp. 15. FISH: Atlantic Manta - 8 (possibly another species, these were very brown and swimming in formation); Ocean Sunfish (1); shark sp. (1); Yellowfin Tune (1 caught); Porbeagle Shark - 1 (rare, our first sighting on these trips).

Observation Details: ***mega; spectacular views and many photos
Checklist Comments: Point at start. First day of BBC Pelagic trip. We motored out past Nantucket and over Nantucket Shoals, which were relatively quiet but produced a nice Great Skua. We reached Hydrographer Canyon before noon and found our first White-faced Storm-Petrels (5!) there. We tried some chumming there and then worked our way offshore into deep water, where we encountered Atlantic Spotted Dolphins but no particularly noteworthy birds. At our most distant and deepest point, we stopped and laid a substantial chum slick and with patience finally had Band-rumped Storm-Petrels arrive. We enjoyed these best-ever studies for more than an hour before pulling up and heading inshore for our overnight anchorage off Welker Canyon. Seas were moderate all day with a moderate NW wind. OTHER FAUNA: FISH: PORBEAGLE SHARK - 1, **rare; seen fairly well off starboard side and photographed; our first for the BBC pelagic trips
BBC trip

Observation Details: extremely distant bird arcing over horizon
Checklist Comments: We spent most of this half-hour admiring the manta ray and its attending fish. Point at start. Second day of BBC Pelagic trip greeted dawn at Welker Canyon with a good chum slick. We then worked our way westward, collecting White-faced Storm-Petrels (especially in Dogbody Canyon). We chummed again in central Hydrographer Canyon, with little result and then motored north back over the Nantucket Shoals and connected with a few more birds as we went. Amazingly, no sizeable shearwater flocks were seen on either day.

Observation Details: Two-day total of 1 (debate continues among leaders; some think Great Skua based on color of some back feathers, others favor South Polar based on body color, back pattern, and primary molt. The photos )
Checklist Comments: Full day total for entire boat for 29 August. No one person saw the entire list of species or complete counts; official tally kept by Rick Heil. OTHER FAUNA: MAMMALS: Fin Whale - 17 (two-day total 23); large whale sp. - 4; Minke Whale - 1; FISH: Atlantic Manta - 2 (great, long close looks); White Suckerfish - 2 (on Atlantic Manta); Pilotfish - 4 (around Atlantic Manta); shark sp. (3); Yellowfin Tune (1 caught); Porbeagle Shark - 1 (rare, our first sighting on these trips).

Observation Details: Distant bird by Luke Seitz only
Checklist Comments: OTHER FAUNA: MAMMALS: Bottlenosed Dolphin - 4, came in to bow briefly. The BBC pelagic trip departed from Hyannis Harbor at 1 am and headed SSW towards some warm water west of Atlantis Canyon. The seas were extremely rough and pushed us a bit west of Atlantis Canyon. The heavy wind made for superb chumming, and birdlife was generally excellent off the Continental Shelf, although the rough seas were hard on many people and made viewing difficult at times. The temperature break was hard to find ultimately, with our warmest sea surface temperatures around 74.5 F. Conditions for the morning were rough seas (4-8 ft, highest swells 10 ft (breaking over bow of Helen H)), overcast skies, wind NE at 10-15 mph, and rather cool (65-75 F).
BBC trip lead by Jonathan Center; Black-Bellied Whistling Duck was a life or state bird for several participants; 72-80 degF, overcast, some wind.

Observation Details: nice group in front of boat
Checklist Comments: Point at start. First day of BBC Pelagic trip. We motored out past Nantucket and over Nantucket Shoals and continued on to Dogbody Canyon. We then ran east along the shelf edge to a nice drop off at Dogbody Canyon, where did a a prolonged chumming stand and got White-faced and Band-rumped Storm-Petrels. Later, we ran further east along the shelf, picked up another White-faced, and then did some more chumming in rather deep water off the southeast corner of Welker Canyon, getting more and better looks at Band-rumped. We ran further east at dusk, picked up a Bridled Tern in the half-light, and anchored just west of Oceanographer Canyon overnight. Seas moderate with 10-15 mph NE wind.

Summary totals for trip. BBC Overnight Pelagic trip, covering about 130 miles back from Hyannis to Dogbody Canyon. BBC pelagic trip with 58 participants including leaders (Marshall Iliff (list-keeper), Jeremiah Trimble, Nick Bonomo, Luke Seitz, Ian Davies, and Mark Faherty). We steamed south over Nantucket Shoals in rough seas and steered for the head of Hydrographer, but deviated slightly east and could not attain Hydrographer. Instead we went to Dogbody Canyon, chummed there, and then motored slowly into the wind and waves, crossing to the east wall before dropping anchor. Seas were extremely rough (5-8 ft) and winds strong ENE. Water temperatures offshore were around 75 F, rising to a peak of around 77 F. OTHER FAUNA: FISH: Whale Shark - 1, photos:

Checklist Comments: BBC Overnight Pelagic trip, covering about 130 miles back from Dogbody Canyon to Hyannis. We anchored overnight at the east wall of Dogbody Canyon in rough seas and chummed for two hours after dawn in 76 F water. Audubon's Shearwaters were streaming past at dawn from south to north. Then we started motoring north, encountering a Red-billed Tropicbird not long after leaving out anchorage. We steamed slowly north, into the swell and waves, through warm water. Seas were extremely rough (5-8 ft) and winds strong ENE. Water temperatures offshore were around 75 F, rising to a peak of around 77 F. We gave ourselves time over the inshore waters to stop for phalaropes and a skua and enjoy some other inshore species.

Location: BBC Pelagic 7 Feb 2016--leg 6 (w of South Shoals Channel)
Observation Details: As we were chasing the fulmar, a distant flyby was spotted flying left across bow by Mike Sylvia showed low long body, gray underwings, and grayish face, giving it a dusky look in flight
Checklist Comments: We pulled away from the large concentration of birds and entered fairly barren waters which had only the occasional kittiwake and gull, and where there were no Razorbills and the the only alcids (save for one distant puffin) were Common Murres. After 10 minutes or so in these waters we picked up a distant Northern Fulmar ahead of us and the captain gave chase, staying n the bird and gaining slowly over a 10-12 minute chase before finally achieving great looks, stopping to chum it in, and having it joined by a second bird. We spent the rest of our time in these waters going more slowly, chumming, and trying to find something different. The water depth here maxed at about 400 ft as we started to slope into the South Shoals Channel. MAMMALS: 2 seal sp. - one possibly photographed by some and seen as we stopped for it due to the long whiskered look that might suggest Harp Seal.    Brookline Bird Club pelagic trip aboard the Helen H captained by Joe Huckemeyer and organized by Ida Giriunas and Naeem Yusuff, which finally sailed after being rescheduled twice. We were wise to wait, since conditions today were perfect for a winter pelagic: mild (40 F), calm with essentially no wind, and clear. We sailed roughly southeast from Hyannis Harbor, taking a track about halfway between the tip of Monomoy and Nantucket. We headed on a course that transected the NE edge of Nantucket Shoals and steered for South Shoals Channel. We encountered a huge concentration of birds (Razorbills, gulls, kittiwakes, and gannets) in an area with whales and a scalloping boat. After ample time to check those birds, we worked our way offshore to deeper water (reaching 400+ ft) and then circled back, checked the birdy zone again, cruised along the edge of South Monomoy, and then beelined back to Hyannis Harbor. on the Seven Seas Whale Watch out of Gloucester, MA.  Cool day at sea,  about 70 degrees F, while it reached 90 degrees on the mainland.  along the way to the N.W.corner of stellwagon banks, we were met by 350 White-sided dolphins, along with several Cory's shearwaters and a gannet. Soon thereafter we met 50 Bottle-nosed dolphins, 2 Minke whales and 2 hump-backed whales...A red-breasted nuthatch fluttered about the boat, decided to hitch a ride with us back to shore...

Prolonged chumming stand at our anchorage, which was just off the east wall of Hydrographer Canyon. Alas, despite deep water nearby we had no deep water specialists here although Wood Duck and Lesser Black-backed Gull were both rarities for the deep water location. Seas were quite rough, estimated by the crew at 7-10 ft with occasional 13 ft swells. Skies were partly cloudy with strong NW winds and at least a few landbirds (flicker!) appeared to be displaced offshore overnight. The crew dip-netted a pufferfish overnight but caught nothing in all-night fishing efforts.    Day two of a two-day BBC Pelagic trip organized by Ida Giriunas, Naeem Yusuff, and Sabrina Hepburn sailing from Hyannis Harbor aboard the Helen H, expertly captained by Joe Huckemeyer. Leaders included Marshall Iliff, Peter Flood, Mike Sylvia, and Liam Waters. We started anchored right off the east wall of Hydrographer Canyon and worked our way north up the center of the canyon and then headed for home since the rough seas promised a slow and challenging slog northward. We found a few concentrations of birds and new species (fulmar) over the Nantucket Shoals and slowed to enjoy those birds there. Seas 6-10 ft offshore (swells to 13 ft) becoming 4-7 ft in the afternoon over the shallower Nantucket Shoals. Water temperatures offshore were in the 72-76° F range (very warm for us) dropping to 65°-59° F range over Nantucket Shoals. There was no evidence of Sargassum weed and the captain confirms that although the water was warm, this was shelf water not water influenced by Gulf Stream eddies, which tended to be further south than normal this year. We arrived back in port a bit bedraggled and about an hour early, thanks partly to the rough seas.

Observation Details: Gyrfalcon - Andrews Point About 10 AM, closest 70 – 100  feet  first seen 75 yards out, at end of Point de Chene.  Came straight in from over the ocean right to left, then moved around us to left. Indirect light.  Overall dark brown appearance, belly was lighter in contrast to the back – tan/creamy with dark stripes on belly.  Large pointy wings, wide at the base – a falcon, dark overall.  Bulky strong large bird (much larger than peregrine also seen at close range on trip).  Helmet type appearance – dark back half of head.  Powerful steady flight.   Came in from SE flying towards NW – Halibut and Lane’s Cove  Seen 30 seconds to 1 minute by six observers.  Dark morph adult

Observation Details: Pans, feeding with flock of SEPLs. Thick breast band, especially at sides of breast, and thick across front of breast. Hint of eye-ring, but for the most part dark / lacking. Black on face meets bill at the gape, and not just above gape as in SEPL (the most reliable field-mark I've used in AK for separating the two). I'm hesitant to call this CRPL as: bill is not particularly long, and neither is the supercilium very bold. I didn't notice any obvious difference in size between this bird and others. Silent and no observation of feet. Poor digiscoped pics.

Brookline Bird Club field trip  Leader Ray Bedard plus 6 participants. Emily, Mimi, Jackie, Bob, Rob and Ben.   Clear sunny skies to start. Temps around 45. Clouds rolled in as the temperature rose to 50 and sprinkled some rain on us.   Sandhill Crane was the main target and they made us work for the sighting. We could hear them multiple times around the south impoundment but could not find them. They called as we were heading back towards the main trail and we decided to do a little bushwhacking to try and see the cranes. The Sandhill’s were spotted by Rob who got us all on the birds.   The wind kept a lot of birds hunkered down and probably kept us from hearing any American Bitterns.   Great trip and I look forward to seeing everyone soon.

Observation Details: ***mega; this small shearwater flew of along the port (left) side of the bow and was initially called out as a small shearwater, possibly a Sargasso Shearwater. Leaders got images from the bow immediately and we gave chase to the bird for the next few minutes, with it tacking left and right at about the eleven o’clock position on the boat, best seen form the front. After a couple minutes the better images were checked and we all agreed it was a confirmed Barolo Shearwater and we doubled-down on the chase, with Captain Joe Huckemeyer stating on the bird as best he could. It sat briefly on the water once, but flushed immediately again. Unfortunately the bird was 10-15% faster than the boat and it gradually slipped away from us. It also seemed to *intentionally* fly into the sun, presumably knowing that a threat from behind (e.g., predator) would have trouble spotting it in the sun (we saw this behavior in 2007 too). It was a real struggle to keep the bird in sight and we eventually lost it in the glare and swell towards the horizon but decided to slow down and approach gradually. This was a good decision, although the tiny shearwater was unfortunately very hard to spot at a distance. By the time we noticed it ahead of us, we only had time to slow the boat and call it out on the water before it flushed again and repeated the chase again: we followed, it flew to 11:00, and flew into the sun, and eventually gained on us and we lost it. This time the slow down and hope to find it on the water did not succeed. In the field it was a small shearwater quite close in size and shape to Audubon’s Shearwater, but maybe with a shorter and less paddle-shaped tail. Its flight was very rapid with quick flaps and short glides and short banking movements. The critical ID criteria rest upon the photos, which clearly show: 1) most importantly, a very white face with the dark eye totally isolated in the white of the face; this is diagnostic for P. baroli; 2) white under tail coverts, which eliminate Sargasso and Boyd’s Shearwater; 3) somewhat silvery sheen to the greater coverts; 4) a narrow line of whitish tips on the greater coverts; this is typical of Barolo and very rare or absent in Sargasso Shearwater. This remains one of the most mysterious birds off New England. Given how hard they are to detect and identify and the fact that there are 10+ records in the past 15 years, it seems likely to be somewhat regular but clearly very rare and thinly distributed. It remains an ABA Code 4 bird (indicating an extreme rarity with 5+ records), and that is appropriate. It is the third ever found on a BBC pelagic trip. The first that we found on these trips was the first United States record (following a specimen from Cape Sable Island and a credible sight record from the Grand Banks off Newfoundland). That first BBC record was 25 Aug 2007 first BBC record was 25 Aug 2007 and was just 21 miles NNW of this bird and in similar water depth and bathymetry
